**Military organization** ([AE](/wiki/American_English "American English")) or **military organisation** ([BE](/wiki/British_English "British English")) is the structuring of the [armed forces](/wiki/Armed_force "Armed force") of a [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") so as to offer such [military capability](/wiki/Military_capability "Military capability") as a [national defense policy](/wiki/Military_policy "Military policy") may require. Formal military organization tends to use [hierarchical](/wiki/Hierarchical "Hierarchical") forms (see [Modern hierarchy](#Modern_hierarchy) for terminology and approximate troop strength per hierarchical unit).

In some countries, [paramilitary](/wiki/Paramilitary "Paramilitary") forces are included in a nation's armed forces, though not considered military. Armed forces that are not a part of military or paramilitary organizations, such as [insurgent](/wiki/Insurgency "Insurgency") forces, often emulate military organizations, or use these structures.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_organization&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

The use of formalized [ranks](/wiki/Military_rank "Military rank") in a hierarchical structure came into widespread use with the [Roman Army](/wiki/Roman_Army "Roman Army").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Military_organization#Dubious "Talk:Military organization")*]The Roman Army was organized into [legions](/wiki/Roman_legion "Roman legion"), each comprising around 5000 soldiers and led by a [legate](/wiki/Legate_(ancient_Rome) "Legate (ancient Rome)"). Each legion was further divided into centuries which were led by [centurions](/wiki/Centurion "Centurion").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

In modern times, executive control, management and administration of military organization is typically undertaken by governments through a [government department](/wiki/Ministry_(government_department) "Ministry (government department)") within the structure of [public administration](/wiki/Public_administration "Public administration"), often known as a  [ministry of defence or department of defense](/wiki/Ministry_of_defence "Ministry of defence"). These in turn manage [military branches](/wiki/Military_branches "Military branches") that themselves command formations and units specialising in combat, [combat support](/wiki/Combat_support "Combat support") and [combat-service support](/wiki/Combat_service_support "Combat service support").

## Executive control, management and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_organization&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Executive control, management and administration")]

The usually [civilian or partly civilian executive control over the national military organization](/wiki/Civilian_control_of_the_military "Civilian control of the military") is exercised in [democracies](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") by an elected political leader as a member of the government's [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(government) "Cabinet (government)"), usually known as a [minister of defence](/wiki/Minister_of_defence "Minister of defence"). In [presidential systems](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system"), such as the [United States](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces"), the president is the [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief"), and the cabinet-level defense minister is second in command. Subordinated to that position are often [secretaries](/wiki/Secretary_(title) "Secretary (title)") for specific major operational divisions of the armed forces as a whole, such as those that provide general support services to the military, including their dependants.

Then there are the heads of specific departmental agencies responsible for the provision and management of specific skill- and knowledge-based services such as [strategic](/wiki/Strategy "Strategy") advice, [capability development](/wiki/Military_capability "Military capability") assessment, or [military science](/wiki/Military_science "Military science") provision of research, and design and development of technologies. Within each departmental agency will be found administrative branches responsible for further agency [business specialization](/wiki/Business "Business") work.

## Military branches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_organization&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Military branches")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/USN-JASDF_ship_and_aircraft_formations_during_ANNUALEX_2008_081119-N-7047S-140.jpg/250px-USN-JASDF_ship_and_aircraft_formations_during_ANNUALEX_2008_081119-N-7047S-140.jpg)](/wiki/File:USN-JASDF_ship_and_aircraft_formations_during_ANNUALEX_2008_081119-N-7047S-140.jpg)

A mixed aircraft and ship formation of military vehicles during an exercise with [USN](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") and [JASDF](/wiki/Japan_Air_Self-Defense_Force "Japan Air Self-Defense Force") vehicles.

In most countries, the *armed forces* are divided into three [military branches](/wiki/Military_branch "Military branch") (also *service, armed service, or military service*): [army](/wiki/Army "Army"), [navy](/wiki/Navy "Navy"), and [air force](/wiki/Air_force "Air force").

Many countries have a variation on the standard model of three basic military branches. Some nations also organize their [cyber force](/wiki/Cyber_force "Cyber force"), emergencies service, [medical service](/wiki/Military_medicine "Military medicine"), [military logistics](/wiki/Military_logistics "Military logistics"), [space force](/wiki/Space_force "Space force"), [marines](/wiki/Marines "Marines"), and [special forces](/wiki/Special_forces "Special forces") such as [commandos](/wiki/Commando "Commando") or [airborne forces](/wiki/Airborne_forces "Airborne forces") as independent armed services. A nation's [border guard](/wiki/Border_guard "Border guard") or [coast guard](/wiki/Coast_guard "Coast guard") may also be an independent branch of its military, although in many nations border guard or coast guard is a civil [law enforcement agency](/wiki/Law_enforcement_agency "Law enforcement agency"). A number of countries have no navy, for geographical reasons such as being [landlocked](/wiki/Landlocked_country "Landlocked country").

In larger armed forces, the cultures between the different branches of the armed forces can be quite different.

Most smaller countries have a single organization that encompasses all armed forces employed by the country in question. Armies of [developing countries](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") tend to consist primarily of [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry"), while [developed countries](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") armies tend to have larger units manning expensive equipment and only a fraction of personnel in infantry units.

In western militaries, a [joint force](/wiki/Joint_warfare "Joint warfare") is defined as a unit or formation comprising combat power from two or more branches of the military.

### Internal security forces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_organization&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Internal security forces")]

[Gendarmeries](/wiki/Gendarmerie "Gendarmerie"), [military police](/wiki/Military_police "Military police") and [security forces](/wiki/Security_forces "Security forces"), including equivalents such as [paramilitary forces](/wiki/Paramilitary "Paramilitary"), [militia](/wiki/Militia "Militia"), [internal troops](/wiki/Internal_Troops "Internal Troops") and [police tactical unit](/wiki/Police_tactical_unit "Police tactical unit"), are an [internal security](/wiki/Internal_security "Internal security") service common in most of the world, but uncommon in countries with English [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") histories where civil police are employed to enforce the law, and there are tight restrictions on how the armed forces may be used to assist.[[a]](#cite_note-2)

## Commands, formations, and units

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_organization&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Commands, formations, and units")]

"Military formation" redirects here. For the arrangement or deployment of moving military forces, see [Tactical formation](/wiki/Tactical_formation "Tactical formation").

It is common, at least in the European and North American militaries, to refer to the building blocks of a military as [commands](/wiki/Command_(military_formation) "Command (military formation)"), **formations**, and **units**.

In a military context, a command is a collection of units and formations under the control of a single officer, although during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") a command was also a name given to a [battlegroup](/wiki/Battlegroup_(army) "Battlegroup (army)") in the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army"). In general, it is an administrative and executive [strategic](/wiki/Military_strategy "Military strategy") headquarters that is responsible to the national government or the national military [headquarters](/wiki/Headquarters "Headquarters"). It is not uncommon for a nation's services to each consist of their own command (such as Land Component, Air Component, Naval Component, and Medical Component in the [Belgian Army](/wiki/Belgian_Army "Belgian Army")), but this does not preclude the existence of commands that are not service-based.

A formation is defined by the [U.S. Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") as "two or more aircraft, ships, or units proceeding together under a commander".[[2]](#cite_note-3) Fomin in the *[Great Soviet Encyclopedia](/wiki/Great_Soviet_Encyclopedia "Great Soviet Encyclopedia")* emphasised its combined-arms nature: "Formations are those military organisations which are formed from different speciality Arms and Services troop units to create a balanced, combined combat force. The formations only differ in their ability to achieve different scales of application of force to achieve different strategic, operational and tactical goals and mission objectives."[[3]](#cite_note-4) It is a composite military organization that includes a mixture of integrated and operationally attached sub-units, and is usually combat-capable. Examples of formations include [divisions](/wiki/Division_(military) "Division (military)"), [brigades](/wiki/Brigade "Brigade"), [battalions](/wiki/Battalion "Battalion"), [wings](/wiki/Wing_(air_force_unit) "Wing (air force unit)"), etc. Formation may also refer to [tactical formation](/wiki/Tactical_formation "Tactical formation"), the physical arrangement or disposition of troops and weapons.[[4]](#cite_note-5) Examples of formation in such usage include [pakfront](/wiki/Pakfront "Pakfront"), [panzerkeil](/wiki/Panzerkeil "Panzerkeil"), [testudo formation](/wiki/Testudo_formation "Testudo formation"), etc.

A typical unit is a homogeneous military organization (either combat, [combat-support](/wiki/Combat_support "Combat support") or non-combat in capability) that includes service personnel predominantly from a single arm of service, or a branch of service, and its administrative and command functions are self-contained. Any unit subordinate to another unit is considered its sub-unit or minor unit. It is not uncommon in the United States for unit and formation to be used synonymously. In [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") practice, formation is not used for smaller organizations such as battalions, which are instead called "units", and their constituent [platoons](/wiki/Platoon "Platoon") or [companies](/wiki/Company_(military_unit) "Company (military unit)") are referred to as sub-units. In the Commonwealth, formations are divisions, brigades, etc.

Different [armed forces](/wiki/Armed_forces "Armed forces"), and even different branches of service of the armed forces, may use the same name to denote different types of organizations. An example is the "squadron". In most navies a [squadron](/wiki/Squadron_(naval) "Squadron (naval)") is a formation of several ships; in most air forces it is a unit; in the U.S. Army it is a battalion-sized [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry") unit; and in Commonwealth armies a squadron is a company-sized sub-unit.

## Table of organization and equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_organization&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Table of organization and equipment")]

A [table of organization and equipment](/wiki/Table_of_organization_and_equipment "Table of organization and equipment") (TOE or TO&E) is a document published by the U.S. Army Force Management Support Agency that prescribes the organization, manning, and equipage of units from divisional size and down, but also including the headquarters of Corps and Armies.

It also provides information on the mission and capabilities of a unit as well as the unit's current status. A general TOE is applicable to a type of unit (for instance, infantry) rather than a specific unit (the 3rd Infantry Division). In this way, all units of the same branch (such as infantry) follow the same structural guidelines.

## Modern hierarchy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_organization&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Modern hierarchy")]

See also: [Command hierarchy](/wiki/Command_hierarchy "Command hierarchy")

### Army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_organization&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Army")]

The following table gives an overview of some of the terms used to describe army hierarchy in armed forces across the world. Whilst it is recognized that there are differences between armies of different nations, many are modeled on the [British](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") or [American](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") models, or both.[[5]](#cite_note-Military_Units:_Army-6) However, many military units and formations go back in history for a long time, and were devised by various military thinkers throughout European history.

For example, the modern *Corps* was first introduced in France about 1805 by Napoleon as a more flexible tactical grouping of two or more divisions during the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars").

Rungs may be skipped in this ladder: for example, typically NATO forces skip from battalion to brigade. Likewise, only large military powers may have organizations at the top levels and different armies and countries may also use traditional names, creating considerable confusion: for example, a British or Canadian armored regiment (battalion) is divided into [squadrons](/wiki/Squadron_(army) "Squadron (army)") (companies) and [troops](/wiki/Troop "Troop") (platoons), whereas an American cavalry squadron (battalion) is divided into troops (companies) and platoons. In the French system (used by many African countries) the company is divided into sections (platoons) composed of 3 x "groupes de combat" of 7 soldiers, plus a group of vehicle crews and a HQ that includes 2 x snipers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Army](/wiki/Army_(unit) "Army (unit)"), [army group](/wiki/Army_group "Army group"), [region](/wiki/Region "Region"), and [theatre](/wiki/Theater_(military) "Theater (military)") are all large formations that vary significantly between armed forces in size and hierarchy position. While [divisions](/wiki/Division_(military) "Division (military)") were the traditional level at which support elements (field artillery, hospital, logistics and maintenance, etc.) were added to the unit structure, since World War II, many brigades now have such support units, and since the 1980s, regiments also have been receiving support elements. A regiment with such support elements is called a [regimental combat team](/wiki/Regimental_combat_team "Regimental combat team") in US military parlance, or a [battlegroup](/wiki/Battlegroup_(army) "Battlegroup (army)") in the UK and other forces. Canadian Army doctrine also includes the [combat team](/wiki/Combat_team "Combat team") which is a company of infantry augmented with tanks, or a squadron of tanks augmented with infantry, or the combination of a full company of infantry with a full squadron of tanks.

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") used the same basic organizational structure. However, in the beginning many units were greatly underpowered and their size was actually one level below on the ladder that is usually used elsewhere; for example, a division in the early-WWII Red Army would have been about the size of most nations' regiments or brigades.[[9]](#cite_note-14)[[10]](#cite_note-15) At the top of the ladder, what other nations would call an army group, the Red Army called a [front](/wiki/Front_(Soviet_Army) "Front (Soviet Army)"). By contrast, during the same period the German [Wehrmacht](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht") army groups, particularly on the [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_II) "Eastern Front (World War II)"), such as [Army Group Centre](/wiki/Army_Group_Centre "Army Group Centre") significantly exceeded the above numbers, and were more cognate with the Soviet [Strategic Directions](/wiki/Strategic_Direction "Strategic Direction").

### Navy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_organization&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Navy")]

Naval organization at the [flotilla](/wiki/Flotilla "Flotilla") level and higher is less commonly abided by, as ships operate in smaller or larger groups in various situations that may change at a moment's notice. However, there is some common terminology used throughout navies to communicate the general concept of how many vessels might be in a unit.

Navies are generally organized into groups for a specific purpose, usually strategic, and these organizational groupings appear and disappear frequently based on the conditions and demands placed upon a navy. This contrasts with army organization where units remain static, with the same men and equipment, over long periods of time.

The five-star ranks of [admiral of the fleet](/wiki/Admiral_of_the_fleet "Admiral of the fleet") and fleet admiral have largely been out of regular use since the 1990s, with the exception of ceremonial or honorary appointments. Currently, all major navies ([blue-water](/wiki/Blue-water_navy "Blue-water navy") and [green-water](/wiki/Green-water_navy "Green-water navy") navies) are commanded by an [admiral](/wiki/Admiral "Admiral") of either four-star rank or three-star rank depending on relative size. Smaller naval forces, such as the [Royal New Zealand Navy](/wiki/Royal_New_Zealand_Navy "Royal New Zealand Navy"), or those navies that are effectively [coast guards](/wiki/Coast_guard "Coast guard"), are commanded by a [rear-admiral](/wiki/Rear-admiral "Rear-admiral") (two-star rank), [commodore](/wiki/Commodore_(rank) "Commodore (rank)") (one-star rank) or even a [captain](/wiki/Captain_(naval) "Captain (naval)").

[Aircraft carriers](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") are typically commanded by a captain. [Submarines](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine") and [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer") are typically commanded by a captain or commander. Some destroyers, particularly smaller destroyers such as [frigates](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate") (formerly known as [destroyer escorts](/wiki/Destroyer_escort "Destroyer escort")) are usually commanded by officers with the rank of commander. [Corvettes](/wiki/Corvette "Corvette"), the smallest class of warship, are commanded by officers with the rank of commander or [lieutenant-commander](/wiki/Lieutenant-commander "Lieutenant-commander"). Auxiliary ships, including [gunboats](/wiki/Gunboat "Gunboat"), [minesweepers](/wiki/Minesweeper_(ship) "Minesweeper (ship)"), [patrol boats](/wiki/Patrol_boat "Patrol boat"), military [riverine craft](/wiki/Riverboat "Riverboat"), [tenders](/wiki/Ship%27s_tender "Ship's tender") and [torpedo boats](/wiki/Torpedo_boat "Torpedo boat") are usually commanded by [lieutenants](/wiki/Lieutenant "Lieutenant"), [sub-lieutenants](/wiki/Sub-lieutenant "Sub-lieutenant") or [warrant officers](/wiki/Warrant_officers "Warrant officers"). Usually, the smaller the vessel, the lower the rank of the ship's commander. For example, [patrol boats](/wiki/Patrol_boat "Patrol boat") are often commanded by [ensigns](/wiki/Ensign_(rank) "Ensign (rank)"), while frigates are rarely commanded by an officer below the rank of commander.

Historically, navies were far more rigid in structure. Ships were collected in [divisions](/wiki/Division_(naval) "Division (naval)"), which in turn were collected in numbered [squadrons](/wiki/Squadron_(naval) "Squadron (naval)"), which comprised a numbered [fleet](/wiki/Naval_fleet "Naval fleet"). Permission for a vessel to leave one unit and join another would have to be approved on paper.

The modern U.S. Navy is primarily based on a number of standard groupings of vessels, including the [carrier strike group](/wiki/Carrier_strike_group "Carrier strike group") and the [expeditionary strike group](/wiki/Expeditionary_strike_group "Expeditionary strike group").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Additionally, naval organization continues aboard a single ship. The complement forms three or four departments (such as tactical and engineering), each of which has a number of divisions, followed by work centers.

### Air force

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_organization&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Air force")]

The organizational structures of air forces vary between nations: some air forces (such as the [United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") and the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force")) are divided into commands, groups and squadrons; others (such as the [Soviet Air Force](/wiki/Soviet_Air_Force "Soviet Air Force")) have an Army-style organizational structure. The modern [Royal Canadian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Air_Force "Royal Canadian Air Force") uses [Air division](/wiki/Air_division "Air division") as the formation between wings and the entire air command. Like the RAF, Canadian wings consist of squadrons.

## Task force

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_organization&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Task force")]

A [task force](/wiki/Task_force "Task force") is a unit or formation created as a temporary grouping for a specific operational purpose. Aside from administrative hierarchical forms of organization that have evolved since the early 17th century in Europe, fighting forces have been grouped for specific operational purposes into mission-related organizations such as the German [Kampfgruppe](/wiki/Kampfgruppe "Kampfgruppe") or the U.S. Combat Team (Army) and Task Force (Navy) during the Second World War, or the Soviet [Operational manoeuvre group](/wiki/Operational_manoeuvre_group "Operational manoeuvre group") during the Cold War. In the British and Commonwealth armies the [battlegroup](/wiki/Battlegroup_(army) "Battlegroup (army)") became the usual grouping of companies during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") and the Cold War.

Within NATO, a [Joint Task Force](/wiki/Joint_Task_Force "Joint Task Force") (JTF) would be such a temporary grouping that includes elements from more than one armed service, a Combined Task Force (CTF) would be such a temporary grouping that includes elements from more than one nation, and a Combined Joint Task Force (CJTF) would be such a temporary grouping that includes elements of more than one armed service and more than one nation.

## Explanatory notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Military_organization&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Explanatory notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** In the United States it is a common misunderstanding that their armed forces are totally prohibited from doing so by the [Posse Comitatus Act](/wiki/Posse_Comitatus_Act "Posse Comitatus Act"). The Act, which reserves to Congress the power to employ federal military force to enforce law and order, refers specifically only to the [US Army](/wiki/US_Army "US Army") and [US Air Force](/wiki/US_Air_Force "US Air Force"). The [US Marines](/wiki/US_Marines "US Marines") and [Navy](/wiki/US_Navy "US Navy") are separately regulated, and the [Coast Guard](/wiki/US_Coast_Guard "US Coast Guard") has a clear law enforcement role in its peacetime status. The state-controlled [Army National Guard](/wiki/Army_National_Guard "Army National Guard") (technically a branch of the US Army) is also excluded from the Posse Comitatus Act. The [Insurrection Act](/wiki/Insurrection_Act "Insurrection Act") specifically permits the president to use federal military force to restore public order in extreme emergency situations: the Act was implemented during the ["Rodney King Riots"](/wiki/1992_Los_Angeles_riots "1992 Los Angeles riots") in Los Angeles.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
